macro_segments.title: Introduction to Shvut: Weekday Activities and Gezeirah Framework

micro_segments.title: Overview of Shvut prohibitions as weekday-type activities versus Babylonian framing as gezeirah
Tosafot's approach to clapping and dancing: reconciling practice with gezeirah reasoning
Extended discussion of medicine on Shabbat: gezeirah reasoning and contemporary applications
Principle of af al pi shebatla hata'am: when reasons disappear but prohibitions remain
Sitting in Beit Din: mitzvah element versus practical non-obligation
Kiddushin on Yom Tov: marriage as enabling mitzvah of pru u'rvu
Tosafot's alternative reading: when mitzvah status should permit Yom Tov activities
Chalitzah and Yibum: older brother priority and quasi-mitzvah status

macro_segments.title: Sanctification and Consecration: Gezeirah of Writing and Business

micro_segments.title: Mekach u'memkar prohibition: Rashi's dual explanation (intrinsic versus gezeirah to writing)
Separation of terumot u'ma'asrot: intrinsic problem or status change prohibition
Giving separated terumah to the kohen: reframing as gift rather than fixing food
Separating challah on Yom Tov: ochel nefesh exception and giving to kohen

macro_segments.title: Conceptual Framework: Reshut versus Mitzvah categories in relation to Shvut

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yitzchak's clarification: all categories involve shvut, with increasing permission levels
Summary of prohibitions: application across Shabbat and Yom Tov with food exception

macro_segments.title: Differences between Shabbat and Yom Tov: Lowering fruit and Beit Shammai versus Beit Hillel

micro_segments.title: Contradiction: mishna claims no differences except food, but previous passage shows fruit-lowering permission
Abuye's resolution: differing conditions of prevention (permitting versus clever tricks)
Rav Papa's resolution: underlying Beit Shammai versus Beit Hillel dispute on carrying and schlepping
Muktza and carrying concern: how Beit Shammai restricts movement to prevent carrying
Gaps in the gemara's resolution: unaddressed differences between Shabbat and Yom Tov

macro_segments.title: Techumim: Objects follow the owner's Techum boundaries

micro_segments.title: Principle: animals and vessels are limited to owner's techum like owner's feet
Giving animal to shepherd or son: whose techum controls the animal
Shared vessels in undivided estate: intersection of techumim for common use items
Borrowing items: timing of possession determines whose techum applies
Woman's dough with borrowed salt and spices: joint techum limitation
Rabbi Yehudah's exemption for water: insubstantial items invisible in mixture

macro_segments.title: Mishnah Analysis: Rabbi Dosa versus Standard Opinion on Animal Ownership

micro_segments.title: Baraita of Rabbi Dosa: purchasing animal before Yom Tov versus actual possession
Giving to shepherd: techum follows shepherd even when transfer occurs on Yom Tov
Gemara reconciliation: one shepherd (assumed recipient) versus two shepherds (choice required)
Rav Yochanan's ruling: halacha like Rabbi Dosa when recipient is predetermined

macro_segments.title: Two Borrowers with Non-Overlapping Techumim: Shirt Cannot Move

micro_segments.title: Scenario: two people borrow shirt with completely opposing eiru'v directions
Rule: item limited to intersection of both owners' techumim
Day-night division irrelevant: Yom Tov techum status fixed for entire day
No overlap scenario: item cannot be moved at all

macro_segments.title: Partnership Property: Barrel versus Animal with Different Techumim

micro_segments.title: Rav's position: barrel can be divided with each owner's half in their own techum
Shmuel's position: both barrel and animal limited to overlapping techumim
Theoretical basis: bereira (retroactive definition) for distinguishing Rav and Shmuel
Animal distinction: organically connected halves derive nutrients from each other
Rav Kahana and Rav Assi challenge: muktza concern versus techum concern analogy
Rav Ushaya's position: yesh bereira allows division to redefine ownership retroactively

macro_segments.title: Contradiction: Rav Ushaya on Bereira in Corpse Tumah Context

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: corpse and multiple exits; only identified exit becomes tamei retroactively
Beit Shammai: thought before death identifies exit; after death thought does not
Beit Hillel: even after death, thought identifies specific exit as tamei
Rav Ushaya's position: definition works forward (mikan u'leha'ba), not retroactively
Apparent contradiction: Rav Ushaya holds ein bereira for corpse but yesh bereira for animals

topical_tags.term: Af al pi shebatla hata'am (reason disappears but prohibition remains)
Ohel nefesh (food-related permissibility on Yom Tov)
